title: Spanish for Beginners: A0 to A2 Complete Course
description: A full beginner Spanish course for English speakers, from absolute zero to A2. 72 lessons of natural, spoken-style Spanish covering greetings, numbers, food, family, directions, shopping, time, weather, past-tense storytelling and making plans. Every sentence is annotated with vocabulary, grammar notes and common idioms real speakers actually use.

lessons.title: Hello and goodbye
lessons.description: Your very first Spanish: greetings, saying your name and saying goodbye.

sentences.text: ¡Hola! Buenos días.
sentences.translation: Hi! Good morning.

chunks.explanation: Literally 'good days'. Used from waking up until around midday.

sentences.text: Me llamo Ana. ¿Y tú? ¿Cómo te llamas?
sentences.translation: My name's Ana. And you? What's your name?

words.explanation: Reflexive verb: literally 'I call myself'.

grammar.explanation: llamarse (to be called) is reflexive. 'Me llamo...' = I'm called...; '¿Cómo te llamas?' = What are you called?

chunks.explanation: Bounces the same question back to the other person.

sentences.text: Me llamo Carlos. Encantado.
sentences.translation: My name's Carlos. Nice to meet you.

words.meaning: pleased to meet you (said by a man)
words.explanation: Literally 'delighted'. A woman says 'encantada'.

sentences.text: Encantada, Carlos. Mucho gusto.
sentences.translation: Nice to meet you, Carlos. A pleasure.

words.meaning: pleased to meet you (said by a woman)

chunks.explanation: Literally 'much pleasure'. Interchangeable with 'encantado/a'.

sentences.text: ¿Cómo estás?
sentences.translation: How are you?

chunks.explanation: The everyday informal 'how are you'. Formal version: '¿Cómo está usted?'

sentences.text: Bien, gracias. ¿Y tú?
sentences.translation: Fine, thanks. And you?

sentences.text: Muy bien, gracias.
sentences.translation: Very well, thanks.

sentences.text: Buenas tardes, señora.
sentences.translation: Good afternoon, ma'am.

chunks.explanation: Used from around midday until dark.

sentences.text: Buenas noches, hasta mañana.
sentences.translation: Good night, see you tomorrow.

chunks.explanation: Both a greeting after dark and a way to say good night.

sentences.text: Adiós, hasta luego.
sentences.translation: Bye, see you later.

chunks.explanation: The most common casual goodbye, even if you won't see the person again soon.

sentences.text: Sí, por favor.
sentences.translation: Yes, please.

sentences.text: No, gracias.
sentences.translation: No, thanks.

sentences.text: Hasta luego, Ana. ¡Adiós!
sentences.translation: See you later, Ana. Bye!

lessons.title: Where are you from?
lessons.description: Say where you're from and ask others, using the verb ser.

sentences.text: Hola, ¿de dónde eres?
sentences.translation: Hi, where are you from?

grammar.explanation: Use 'ser de' to talk about origin. Literally 'from where are you?'

sentences.text: Soy de México. Soy mexicano.
sentences.translation: I'm from Mexico. I'm Mexican.

words.explanation: Nationalities are not capitalised in Spanish.

grammar.explanation: 'ser de' + a place tells where someone or something is from.

sentences.text: ¿Y tú? ¿Eres de España?
sentences.translation: And you? Are you from Spain?

sentences.text: No, no soy de España. Soy de Estados Unidos.
sentences.translation: No, I'm not from Spain. I'm from the United States.

grammar.explanation: Put 'no' right before the verb to make it negative.

sentences.text: Ah, eres estadounidense. ¿De qué ciudad?
sentences.translation: Ah, you're American. From what city?

words.explanation: Same form for men and women.

sentences.text: Soy de Chicago, pero ahora vivo en Madrid.
sentences.translation: I'm from Chicago, but now I live in Madrid.

chunks.explanation: 'vivir en' + a place = to live in a place.

sentences.text: ¡Qué bien! Yo también soy de Madrid.
sentences.translation: How nice! I'm from Madrid too.

sentences.text: ¿Es un país grande?
sentences.translation: Is it a big country?

sentences.text: Sí, México es un país muy grande.
sentences.translation: Yes, Mexico is a very big country.

sentences.text: ¿Tú también eres estudiante?
sentences.translation: Are you a student too?

sentences.text: Sí, soy estudiante. Estudio español.
sentences.translation: Yes, I'm a student. I study Spanish.

sentences.text: Pues, ¡bienvenido a España!
sentences.translation: Well, welcome to Spain!

words.explanation: A very common filler to start a reply.

lessons.title: This is my friend
lessons.description: Introduce other people using subject pronouns and the full present of ser.

sentences.text: Mira, este es mi amigo Pablo.
sentences.translation: Look, this is my friend Pablo.

words.explanation: Used here as 'hey, look' to grab attention.

grammar.explanation: Use 'este es' for a male and 'esta es' for a female when introducing someone.

sentences.text: Hola, Pablo. Mucho gusto. Yo soy Ana.
sentences.translation: Hi, Pablo. Nice to meet you. I'm Ana.

words.explanation: Optional in Spanish; used here for emphasis.

sentences.text: Y esta es mi amiga Lucía. Ella es profesora.
sentences.translation: And this is my friend Lucía. She's a teacher.

sentences.text: ¿Él también es profesor?
sentences.translation: Is he also a teacher?

sentences.text: No, él es estudiante, como yo.
sentences.translation: No, he's a student, like me.

sentences.text: Nosotros somos compañeros de clase.
sentences.translation: We're classmates.

grammar.explanation: 'somos' is the 'nosotros' (we) form of ser.

sentences.text: ¿Y ellos? ¿Quiénes son?
sentences.translation: And them? Who are they?

sentences.text: Ellos son los profesores de la escuela.
sentences.translation: They're the school's teachers.

sentences.text: El señor Gómez es muy simpático.
sentences.translation: Mr. Gómez is very nice.

sentences.text: La señora Ruiz también es profesora nuestra.
sentences.translation: Mrs. Ruiz is also a teacher of ours.

sentences.text: Pues encantada de conoceros a todos.
sentences.translation: Well, nice to meet you all.

chunks.explanation: 'conoceros' = to meet you (plural, Spain). In Latin America you'd say 'conocerlos'.

sentences.text: El gusto es nuestro.
sentences.translation: The pleasure is ours.

chunks.explanation: A polite reply to 'nice to meet you'.

lessons.title: The house and the table
lessons.description: Everyday objects with their articles: learning noun gender.

sentences.text: Esta es la casa de mi familia.
sentences.translation: This is my family's house.

grammar.explanation: Most nouns ending in -a are feminine and take 'la'.

sentences.text: En la cocina hay una mesa y cuatro sillas.
sentences.translation: In the kitchen there's a table and four chairs.

grammar.explanation: 'un' goes with masculine nouns, 'una' with feminine ones.

sentences.text: El libro está encima de la mesa.
sentences.translation: The book is on the table.

grammar.explanation: Most nouns ending in -o are masculine and take 'el'.

sentences.text: Hay un coche delante de la casa.
sentences.translation: There's a car in front of the house.

sentences.text: El perro y el gato están en el jardín.
sentences.translation: The dog and the cat are in the garden.

sentences.text: ¿Dónde está mi teléfono?
sentences.translation: Where's my phone?

sentences.text: Tu teléfono está al lado del bolso.
sentences.translation: Your phone is next to the bag.

sentences.text: ¿Y las llaves? No encuentro las llaves.
sentences.translation: And the keys? I can't find the keys.

sentences.text: Están cerca de la puerta.
sentences.translation: They're near the door.

sentences.text: La ventana está abierta.
sentences.translation: The window is open.

sentences.text: ¿Es un problema?
sentences.translation: Is it a problem?

words.explanation: Ends in -a but is masculine: 'el problema', 'un problema'. Many words from Greek behave this way.

sentences.text: No, no es ninguna cosa importante.
sentences.translation: No, it's nothing important.

lessons.title: Numbers and how many
lessons.description: Count from zero to twenty and ask how many there are.

sentences.text: Uno, dos, tres, cuatro, cinco.
sentences.translation: One, two, three, four, five.

sentences.text: Seis, siete, ocho, nueve, diez.
sentences.translation: Six, seven, eight, nine, ten.

sentences.text: ¿Cuántos años tienes? Tengo once.
sentences.translation: How old are you? I'm eleven.

grammar.explanation: 'cuántos' before masculine plurals, 'cuántas' before feminine plurals.

sentences.text: Mi hermana tiene doce años.
sentences.translation: My sister is twelve.

sentences.text: ¿Cuántas sillas hay en la clase?
sentences.translation: How many chairs are there in the class?

grammar.explanation: 'hay' means both 'there is' and 'there are' and never changes form.

sentences.text: Hay veinte sillas.
sentences.translation: There are twenty chairs.

sentences.text: ¿Y cuántos libros hay?
sentences.translation: And how many books are there?

sentences.text: Hay quince libros y cinco cuadernos.
sentences.translation: There are fifteen books and five notebooks.

sentences.text: Mi número de teléfono es el trece, catorce, dieciséis.
sentences.translation: My phone number is thirteen, fourteen, sixteen.

sentences.text: Hay diecisiete estudiantes hoy.
sentences.translation: There are seventeen students today.

sentences.text: ¿Dieciocho o diecinueve?
sentences.translation: Eighteen or nineteen?

sentences.text: Cero problemas, todo perfecto.
sentences.translation: Zero problems, all perfect.

lessons.title: Where is the bathroom?
lessons.description: Ask and say where things are, using estar and prepositions of place.

sentences.text: Perdona, ¿dónde está el baño?
sentences.translation: Excuse me, where's the bathroom?

words.explanation: Informal 'excuse me' to get attention. Formal: 'perdone'.

grammar.explanation: Use 'estar', not 'ser', to say where something is located.

sentences.text: Está allí, al lado de la cocina.
sentences.translation: It's over there, next to the kitchen.

sentences.text: Gracias. ¿Y la cocina está aquí?
sentences.translation: Thanks. And the kitchen is here?

sentences.text: Sí, la cocina está cerca, a la derecha.
sentences.translation: Yes, the kitchen is nearby, on the right.

sentences.text: ¿La habitación está lejos?
sentences.translation: Is the room far?

sentences.text: No, no está lejos. Está al final del pasillo.
sentences.translation: No, it's not far. It's at the end of the hall.

sentences.text: El gato está debajo de la mesa.
sentences.translation: The cat is under the table.

sentences.text: Y el libro está encima de la silla.
sentences.translation: And the book is on top of the chair.

sentences.text: ¿Dónde estás tú ahora?
sentences.translation: Where are you now?

sentences.text: Estoy en la calle, cerca de tu casa.
sentences.translation: I'm on the street, near your house.

sentences.text: Vale, la puerta está abierta. Entra.
sentences.translation: Okay, the door's open. Come in.

words.explanation: Very common in Spain for 'okay/alright'.

sentences.text: Perfecto, ya estoy aquí.
sentences.translation: Perfect, I'm here now.

lessons.title: How are you feeling?
lessons.description: Talk about moods and states with estar, and learn when to use ser instead.

sentences.text: Hola, ¿qué tal? ¿Cómo estás hoy?
sentences.translation: Hi, how's it going? How are you today?

chunks.explanation: A casual 'how are you / how's it going'.

sentences.text: Estoy un poco cansado, la verdad.
sentences.translation: I'm a bit tired, honestly.

sentences.text: ¿Por qué? ¿Estás enfermo?
sentences.translation: Why? Are you sick?

sentences.text: No, solo estoy muy ocupado con el trabajo.
sentences.translation: No, I'm just very busy with work.

grammar.explanation: Use 'estar' for temporary states like being busy, tired or happy.

sentences.text: Yo estoy muy contenta. ¡Es viernes!
sentences.translation: I'm very happy. It's Friday!

sentences.text: Ana siempre está tranquila y nunca está nerviosa.
sentences.translation: Ana is always calm and never nervous.

sentences.text: Es que ella es una persona muy tranquila.
sentences.translation: It's that she's a very calm person.

grammar.surface: ella es una persona muy tranquila
grammar.meaning: she is a very calm person
grammar.explanation: Use 'ser' for permanent traits (who someone is), 'estar' for how they feel right now.

chunks.meaning: the thing is, it's just that

sentences.text: Pablo está triste porque está aburrido.
sentences.translation: Pablo is sad because he's bored.

words.explanation: 'estar aburrido' = to be bored; 'ser aburrido' = to be boring.

sentences.text: ¿Y tú? ¿Estás enfadada conmigo?
sentences.translation: And you? Are you angry with me?

sentences.text: No, tranquilo. Estoy bien, más o menos.
sentences.translation: No, don't worry. I'm fine, more or less.

sentences.text: Bueno, ¿estamos listos para salir?
sentences.translation: Well, are we ready to go out?

words.explanation: 'estar listo' = to be ready; 'ser listo' = to be clever.

sentences.text: Sí, todo bien. Vamos.
sentences.translation: Yes, all good. Let's go.

lessons.title: More numbers and plurals
lessons.description: Count up to a hundred and make nouns plural.

sentences.text: Veintiuno, treinta, cuarenta, cincuenta.
sentences.translation: Twenty-one, thirty, forty, fifty.

sentences.text: Sesenta, setenta, ochenta, noventa, cien.
sentences.translation: Sixty, seventy, eighty, ninety, a hundred.

sentences.text: En la clase hay muchos estudiantes.
sentences.translation: In the class there are many students.

grammar.explanation: Nouns ending in a vowel add '-s' to become plural.

sentences.text: Hay treinta y dos mesas y varios ordenadores.
sentences.translation: There are thirty-two tables and several computers.

words.explanation: 'ordenador' in Spain; 'computadora' in Latin America.

grammar.explanation: Nouns ending in a consonant add '-es' in the plural: ordenador → ordenadores.

sentences.text: Pocos profesores hablan tres idiomas.
sentences.translation: Few teachers speak three languages.

sentences.text: ¿Cuánto cuesta? Cuesta cuarenta y cinco euros.
sentences.translation: How much is it? It's forty-five euros.

sentences.text: Es un poco caro. Tengo setenta y cinco dólares.
sentences.translation: It's a bit expensive. I have seventy-five dollars.

sentences.text: Mi abuelo tiene noventa años.
sentences.translation: My grandfather is ninety years old.

sentences.text: ¡Noventa! ¿De verdad? ¡Qué mayor!
sentences.translation: Ninety! Really? How old!

sentences.text: Hay cien personas en la fiesta.
sentences.translation: There are a hundred people at the party.

sentences.text: ¿Cuál es tu edad? Tengo veintiocho años.
sentences.translation: What's your age? I'm twenty-eight.

sentences.text: Somos ochenta y cinco en total.
sentences.translation: We're eighty-five in total.

lessons.title: I work and I study
lessons.description: Talk about what you do every day with regular -ar verbs.

sentences.text: Yo trabajo en una oficina.
sentences.translation: I work in an office.

grammar.explanation: Regular -ar verbs: yo -o, tú -as, él/ella -a. So trabajar → trabajo, trabajas, trabaja.

sentences.text: ¿Y tú? ¿Dónde trabajas?
sentences.translation: And you? Where do you work?

sentences.text: No trabajo. Estudio en la universidad.
sentences.translation: I don't work. I study at university.

sentences.text: Ana estudia inglés y también habla francés.
sentences.translation: Ana studies English and also speaks French.

sentences.text: Por la mañana escucho música y tomo café.
sentences.translation: In the morning I listen to music and have coffee.

words.explanation: 'tomar' is used for eating/drinking: tomar un café, tomar algo.

sentences.text: Después cocino algo y descanso un poco.
sentences.translation: Then I cook something and rest a little.

sentences.text: ¿Necesitas algo del supermercado?
sentences.translation: Do you need anything from the supermarket?

sentences.text: Sí, necesito comprar pan y leche.
sentences.translation: Yes, I need to buy bread and milk.

sentences.text: Nosotros compramos la comida los sábados.
sentences.translation: We buy the food on Saturdays.

grammar.explanation: The 'nosotros' (we) form of -ar verbs ends in -amos.

sentences.text: Los niños miran la televisión.
sentences.translation: The children watch television.

sentences.text: ¿Usas mucho el móvil?
sentences.translation: Do you use your phone a lot?

sentences.text: No mucho. Deseo descansar más.
sentences.translation: Not much. I want to rest more.

lessons.title: I eat and I live here
lessons.description: Regular -er and -ir verbs for more everyday actions.

sentences.text: Yo como en casa todos los días.
sentences.translation: I eat at home every day.

grammar.explanation: Regular -er verbs: comer → como, comes, come, comemos, comen.

sentences.text: ¿Bebes café o té por la mañana?
sentences.translation: Do you drink coffee or tea in the morning?

sentences.text: Bebo té. Y leo el periódico.
sentences.translation: I drink tea. And I read the newspaper.

sentences.text: Mi hermana lee una novela nueva.
sentences.translation: My sister is reading a new novel.

sentences.text: Nosotros aprendemos español y comprendemos mucho.
sentences.translation: We're learning Spanish and we understand a lot.

sentences.text: ¿Dónde vives ahora?
sentences.translation: Where do you live now?

grammar.explanation: Regular -ir verbs look like -er verbs except 'nosotros': vivir → vivo, vives, vive, vivimos, viven.

sentences.text: Vivo en un piso pequeño en el centro.
sentences.translation: I live in a small flat in the centre.

sentences.text: Escribo muchos mensajes a mis amigos.
sentences.translation: I write a lot of messages to my friends.

sentences.text: A veces recibo una carta o un correo.
sentences.translation: Sometimes I get a letter or an email.

sentences.text: El camarero abre la puerta a las nueve.
sentences.translation: The waiter opens the door at nine.

sentences.text: Los niños corren en el parque.
sentences.translation: The children run in the park.

sentences.text: Esa tienda vende libros y también vende café.
sentences.translation: That shop sells books and also sells coffee.

lessons.title: How old are you?
lessons.description: Use tener for age, possession and physical needs like hunger and thirst.

sentences.text: ¿Cuántos años tienes?
sentences.translation: How old are you?

grammar.explanation: Spanish uses 'tener' (to have) for age: 'tengo 30 años' = I'm 30, literally 'I have 30 years'.

sentences.text: Tengo treinta años. ¿Y tú?
sentences.translation: I'm thirty. And you?

sentences.text: Yo tengo veinticinco. Mi hermano tiene diez.
sentences.translation: I'm twenty-five. My brother is ten.

sentences.text: Tengo mucha hambre. ¿Comemos algo?
sentences.translation: I'm really hungry. Shall we eat something?

grammar.explanation: Spanish uses 'tener' for many states: tener hambre (be hungry), tener sed (be thirsty), tener sueño (be sleepy).

sentences.text: Yo no tengo hambre, pero tengo mucha sed.
sentences.translation: I'm not hungry, but I'm really thirsty.

sentences.text: El niño tiene sueño y también tiene frío.
sentences.translation: The child is sleepy and also cold.

sentences.text: En verano siempre tengo calor.
sentences.translation: In summer I'm always hot.

sentences.text: Tenemos prisa. El tren sale pronto.
sentences.translation: We're in a hurry. The train leaves soon.

sentences.text: ¿Tienes hermanos?
sentences.translation: Do you have any siblings?

sentences.text: Sí, tengo una hermana. No tengo hermanos.
sentences.translation: Yes, I have one sister. I don't have any brothers.

sentences.text: ¿Tienes dinero para el café?
sentences.translation: Do you have money for the coffee?

sentences.text: No tengo mucho, pero tengo tiempo.
sentences.translation: I don't have much, but I have time.

lessons.title: My family
lessons.description: Introduce your family and show possession with mi, tu and su.

sentences.text: Esta es una foto de mi familia.
sentences.translation: This is a photo of my family.

sentences.text: Mi padre se llama Juan y mi madre se llama Rosa.
sentences.translation: My father's name is Juan and my mother's is Rosa.

grammar.explanation: 'mi' means 'my' for one thing; 'mis' for more than one: mi hijo, mis hijos.

sentences.text: Mis padres tienen tres hijos.
sentences.translation: My parents have three children.

sentences.text: Mi hijo es el mayor y mi hija es la menor.
sentences.translation: My son is the oldest and my daughter is the youngest.

sentences.text: ¿Cómo se llama tu abuela?
sentences.translation: What's your grandmother's name?

grammar.explanation: 'tu' (no accent) = your; 'tú' (with accent) = you.

sentences.text: Mi abuela se llama Carmen y mi abuelo, Pedro.
sentences.translation: My grandmother's name is Carmen, and my grandfather, Pedro.

sentences.text: Este es el hijo de mi tía, mi primo Diego.
sentences.translation: This is my aunt's son, my cousin Diego.

grammar.explanation: Spanish has no apostrophe-s. Use 'de': 'el coche de Ana' = Ana's car.

sentences.text: Su padre, mi tío, es muy simpático.
sentences.translation: His father, my uncle, is very nice.

grammar.explanation: 'su' covers his, her, its and their. Context tells you which.

sentences.text: Mi esposa y yo tenemos dos gatos.
sentences.translation: My wife and I have two cats.

sentences.text: Y tú, ¿tienes esposo o novia?
sentences.translation: And you, do you have a husband or a girlfriend?

sentences.text: Tengo novio. Sus padres viven en Sevilla.
sentences.translation: I have a boyfriend. His parents live in Seville.

sentences.text: ¡Qué familia tan grande y bonita!
sentences.translation: What a big, lovely family!

chunks.explanation: '¡Qué + noun + tan + adjective!' is a common way to exclaim about something.

lessons.title: I like coffee
lessons.description: Say what you like and dislike with the special verb gustar.

sentences.text: Me gusta el café por la mañana.
sentences.translation: I like coffee in the morning.

grammar.explanation: 'gustar' works backwards: literally 'coffee is pleasing to me'. Use 'gusta' with one thing.

sentences.text: ¿Te gusta el té?
sentences.translation: Do you like tea?

grammar.explanation: 'me gusta' = I like; 'te gusta' = you like; 'le gusta' = he/she likes.

sentences.text: No, no me gusta el té. Prefiero el chocolate.
sentences.translation: No, I don't like tea. I prefer chocolate.

sentences.text: Me gustan mucho las frutas.
sentences.translation: I really like fruit.

grammar.explanation: With plural things, use 'gustan': me gustan las manzanas.

sentences.text: A mí también me gustan las verduras.
sentences.translation: I like vegetables too.

chunks.explanation: Used to agree with a positive statement. To agree with a negative one, say 'a mí tampoco'.

sentences.text: A Ana le gusta mucho el deporte.
sentences.translation: Ana really likes sports.

grammar.explanation: Name the person with 'a': 'A Ana le gusta...' = Ana likes...

sentences.text: Le gusta nadar y también le gusta bailar.
sentences.translation: She likes swimming and she also likes dancing.

grammar.explanation: With an action, use 'gusta' + the infinitive verb, even for several actions.

sentences.text: A mí me gusta cantar, pero canto fatal.
sentences.translation: I like singing, but I sing terribly.

words.explanation: Colloquial for 'really badly'.

sentences.text: Nos gusta mucho el cine los domingos.
sentences.translation: We really like the cinema on Sundays.

grammar.explanation: 'nos gusta' = we like; 'les gusta' = they like.

sentences.text: Me encanta viajar por el mundo.
sentences.translation: I love travelling around the world.

words.explanation: 'encantar' is stronger than 'gustar' and works the same way.

sentences.text: ¿Qué tipo de comida te gusta más?
sentences.translation: What kind of food do you like most?

sentences.text: Me gusta todo, la verdad.
sentences.translation: I like everything, honestly.

lessons.title: Colours and descriptions
lessons.description: Describe things with colours and adjectives that agree with the noun.

sentences.text: Tengo un coche rojo y una casa blanca.
sentences.translation: I have a red car and a white house.

grammar.explanation: Adjectives match the noun: 'coche rojo' (masc.), 'casa blanca' (fem.).

sentences.text: El cielo es azul y la hierba es verde.
sentences.translation: The sky is blue and the grass is green.

words.explanation: Colours ending in -e or a consonant don't change for gender: coche verde, casa verde.

sentences.text: Mi camiseta amarilla es nueva.
sentences.translation: My yellow T-shirt is new.

sentences.text: Los zapatos negros son un poco viejos.
sentences.translation: The black shoes are a bit old.

grammar.explanation: Plural nouns take plural adjectives: 'zapatos negros', 'casas blancas'.

sentences.text: Esta casa es muy grande y muy bonita.
sentences.translation: This house is very big and very pretty.

grammar.explanation: 'muy' means 'very' and goes right before an adjective.

sentences.text: Aquel edificio es feo y muy alto.
sentences.translation: That building is ugly and very tall.

sentences.text: Mi hermano es alto, pero yo soy bajo.
sentences.translation: My brother is tall, but I'm short.

sentences.text: Tu perro es pequeño y muy guapo.
sentences.translation: Your dog is small and very cute.

words.explanation: Used for people and, informally, for pets.

sentences.text: ¿De qué color es tu bolso nuevo?
sentences.translation: What colour is your new bag?

sentences.text: Es negro con flores rojas.
sentences.translation: It's black with red flowers.

sentences.text: Me gusta mucho. Es un bolso precioso.
sentences.translation: I really like it. It's a gorgeous bag.

sentences.text: Gracias, es mi color favorito.
sentences.translation: Thanks, it's my favourite colour.

lessons.title: Days and months
lessons.description: Days of the week, months and how to say the date.

sentences.text: Hoy es lunes y mañana es martes.
sentences.translation: Today is Monday and tomorrow is Tuesday.

words.explanation: Days are not capitalised in Spanish.

sentences.text: El miércoles y el jueves trabajo mucho.
sentences.translation: On Wednesday and Thursday I work a lot.

grammar.explanation: 'el lunes' = on Monday; 'los lunes' = on Mondays.

sentences.text: El viernes por la tarde salgo con amigos.
sentences.translation: On Friday afternoon I go out with friends.

sentences.text: El sábado y el domingo son el fin de semana.
sentences.translation: Saturday and Sunday are the weekend.

sentences.text: ¿Qué día es hoy? Es el cinco de enero.
sentences.translation: What day is it today? It's the fifth of January.

grammar.explanation: Dates use plain numbers: 'el 5 de enero'. Only the first is sometimes 'el primero'.

sentences.text: Mi cumpleaños es el diez de febrero.
sentences.translation: My birthday is the tenth of February.

sentences.text: En marzo empieza la primavera.
sentences.translation: In March spring begins.

sentences.text: Tengo vacaciones en agosto.
sentences.translation: I have holidays in August.

sentences.text: ¿Cuántas semanas hay en un mes?
sentences.translation: How many weeks are there in a month?

sentences.text: Un mes tiene cuatro semanas más o menos.
sentences.translation: A month has about four weeks.

sentences.text: Ayer fue domingo, un día tranquilo.
sentences.translation: Yesterday was Sunday, a quiet day.

sentences.text: ¡Qué rápido pasa la semana!
sentences.translation: How fast the week goes by!

lessons.title: What time is it?
lessons.description: Tell the time and talk about your daily schedule.

sentences.text: ¿Qué hora es?
sentences.translation: What time is it?

grammar.explanation: Fixed question for the time. The answer uses 'es la...' or 'son las...'.

sentences.text: Es la una en punto.
sentences.translation: It's one o'clock.

grammar.explanation: Use 'es la una' only for one o'clock; every other hour uses 'son las...'.

sentences.text: Son las tres y cuarto.
sentences.translation: It's a quarter past three.

grammar.explanation: 'y cuarto' = quarter past; 'y media' = half past; 'menos cuarto' = quarter to.

sentences.text: Son las siete y media de la tarde.
sentences.translation: It's half past seven in the evening.

sentences.text: Son las nueve menos cuarto de la mañana.
sentences.translation: It's a quarter to nine in the morning.

sentences.text: La clase empieza a las diez.
sentences.translation: The class starts at ten.

grammar.explanation: Use 'a la una' / 'a las dos' to say at what time something happens.

sentences.text: Y termina a las doce del mediodía.
sentences.translation: And it ends at twelve noon.

sentences.text: Tengo una cita con el médico por la tarde.
sentences.translation: I have an appointment with the doctor in the afternoon.

sentences.text: Por la noche descanso y leo un rato.
sentences.translation: At night I rest and read for a while.

sentences.text: ¿A qué hora te levantas? Muy temprano.
sentences.translation: What time do you get up? Very early.

sentences.text: A medianoche todavía miro el reloj.
sentences.translation: At midnight I'm still looking at the clock.

sentences.text: ¡Es tarde! Tenemos que dormir.
sentences.translation: It's late! We have to sleep.

lessons.title: Asking questions
lessons.description: The main question words and how to build questions.

sentences.text: ¿Qué haces los fines de semana?
sentences.translation: What do you do at weekends?

grammar.explanation: In questions, qué, quién, dónde, cómo, cuándo carry a written accent.

sentences.text: ¿Quién es esa persona?
sentences.translation: Who is that person?

sentences.text: ¿Dónde está la estación?
sentences.translation: Where is the station?

sentences.text: ¿Cuándo empieza la película?
sentences.translation: When does the film start?

sentences.text: ¿Cómo se dice esto en español?
sentences.translation: How do you say this in Spanish?

chunks.explanation: '¿Cómo se dice...?' is the go-to phrase for asking how to say something.

sentences.text: ¿Por qué estudias español?
sentences.translation: Why do you study Spanish?

sentences.text: Estudio español porque me gusta mucho.
sentences.translation: I study Spanish because I really like it.

grammar.explanation: '¿por qué?' (two words, accent) asks why; 'porque' (one word) answers because.

sentences.text: ¿Cuál es tu número de teléfono?
sentences.translation: What's your phone number?

words.explanation: Use 'cuál' when choosing from options; 'qué' for definitions.

sentences.text: ¿Cuánto cuesta esta pregunta?
sentences.translation: How much does this question cost?

sentences.text: No sé la respuesta. No entiendo bien.
sentences.translation: I don't know the answer. I don't understand well.

sentences.text: Creo que sí, pero no estoy seguro.
sentences.translation: I think so, but I'm not sure.

sentences.text: ¿Qué piensas tú de todo esto?
sentences.translation: What do you think about all this?

lessons.title: I want to order
lessons.description: Order food and drink at a café using querer.

sentences.text: Buenos días. ¿Qué quiere tomar?
sentences.translation: Good morning. What would you like to have?

grammar.explanation: 'querer' changes its stem: quiero, quieres, quiere, but queremos, quieren.

sentences.text: Quiero un café con leche, por favor.
sentences.translation: I'd like a coffee with milk, please.

sentences.text: ¿Quieres algo de comer también?
sentences.translation: Do you want something to eat too?

sentences.text: Sí, quiero un bocadillo de tortilla.
sentences.translation: Yes, I'd like a Spanish omelette sandwich.

words.explanation: In Spain, 'tortilla' is a potato omelette, not a flatbread.

sentences.text: El camarero pregunta qué quiere el señor.
sentences.translation: The waiter asks what the gentleman wants.

sentences.text: Para mí, una cerveza bien fría.
sentences.translation: For me, a nice cold beer.

chunks.explanation: Handy for ordering: 'para mí, una cerveza'.

sentences.text: ¿Tienen zumo de naranja natural?
sentences.translation: Do you have fresh orange juice?

words.explanation: 'zumo' in Spain; 'jugo' in Latin America.

sentences.text: ¿Me trae el menú, por favor?
sentences.translation: Could you bring me the menu, please?

sentences.text: Todo está muy rico y muy caliente.
sentences.translation: Everything is delicious and very hot.

sentences.text: ¿Nos trae la cuenta, por favor?
sentences.translation: Could you bring us the bill, please?

sentences.text: Aquí tiene. La propina no está incluida.
sentences.translation: Here you go. The tip isn't included.

chunks.meaning: here you are, here you go

sentences.text: Muchas gracias. Todo perfecto.
sentences.translation: Thank you very much. Everything perfect.

lessons.title: At the market
lessons.description: Shop for food at the market using hay and quantities.

sentences.text: En el mercado hay mucha fruta fresca.
sentences.translation: At the market there's a lot of fresh fruit.

sentences.text: ¿Hay manzanas? Sí, hay muchas.
sentences.translation: Are there apples? Yes, there are lots.

grammar.explanation: '¿hay...?' asks if something exists; 'no hay' says there isn't/aren't.

sentences.text: Quiero un kilo de naranjas, por favor.
sentences.translation: I'd like a kilo of oranges, please.

grammar.explanation: Use 'de' to link a quantity and the food: un kilo de, una botella de.

sentences.text: No hay pan hoy. Se acabó.
sentences.translation: There's no bread today. It's sold out.

sentences.text: Necesito una botella de leche y una docena de huevos.
sentences.translation: I need a bottle of milk and a dozen eggs.

sentences.text: ¿Cuánto cuesta el queso? Es muy barato.
sentences.translation: How much is the cheese? It's very cheap.

sentences.text: La carne está un poco cara hoy.
sentences.translation: The meat is a bit expensive today.

sentences.text: ¿Tienen pescado fresco?
sentences.translation: Do you have fresh fish?

sentences.text: Sí, y también hay arroz y verduras.
sentences.translation: Yes, and there's also rice and vegetables.

sentences.text: Póngame medio kilo de tomates.
sentences.translation: Give me half a kilo of tomatoes.

chunks.explanation: Very common when buying: literally 'put me'. Polite and normal at a market.

sentences.text: ¿Algo más? No, nada más, gracias.
sentences.translation: Anything else? No, that's all, thanks.

sentences.text: Son ocho euros en total. Aquí tiene.
sentences.translation: That's eight euros in total. Here you go.

lessons.title: A morning at the café
lessons.description: A0 review: greetings, ordering and small talk woven together.

sentences.text: Buenos días. ¿Qué tal? ¿Un desayuno?
sentences.translation: Good morning. How's it going? Breakfast?

sentences.text: Sí, por favor. Quiero un café y un croissant.
sentences.translation: Yes, please. I'd like a coffee and a croissant.

sentences.text: ¿Con azúcar? Sí, un poco, gracias.
sentences.translation: With sugar? Yes, a little, thanks.

sentences.text: Claro. Aquí tiene una servilleta.
sentences.translation: Of course. Here's a napkin.

sentences.text: Perdón, el vaso está sucio. ¿Otro, por favor?
sentences.translation: Sorry, the glass is dirty. Another one, please?

sentences.text: Vale, un momento. Ahora traigo otro.
sentences.translation: Okay, one moment. I'll bring another now.

sentences.text: El café está muy caliente y muy rico.
sentences.translation: The coffee is very hot and delicious.

sentences.text: ¿Está todo bien? Sí, perfecto, gracias.
sentences.translation: Is everything okay? Yes, perfect, thanks.

sentences.text: Entonces, ¿la taza es para llevar?
sentences.translation: So, is the cup to take away?

sentences.text: No, tomo el café aquí. Tengo tiempo.
sentences.translation: No, I'll have the coffee here. I have time.

sentences.text: ¿Me pone la cuenta otra vez, por favor?
sentences.translation: Can you give me the bill again, please?

sentences.text: Aquí tiene. ¡Que tenga un buen día!
sentences.translation: Here you go. Have a nice day!

chunks.explanation: Formal, warm goodbye. Informal version: '¡que tengas un buen día!'

lessons.title: I'm going to the centre
lessons.description: Say where you're going with the verb ir and get around town.

sentences.text: ¿Adónde vas con tanta prisa?
sentences.translation: Where are you off to in such a hurry?

grammar.explanation: 'ir' is irregular: voy, vas, va, vamos, van. It rarely appears without 'a'.

sentences.text: Voy al centro. Tengo que darme prisa.
sentences.translation: I'm going downtown. I have to hurry.

grammar.explanation: 'a' + 'el' always contracts to 'al': voy al parque, voy al banco.

chunks.explanation: From 'darse prisa', literally 'to give oneself hurry' = to hurry. Very common, changes with the person: date prisa (hurry up!).

sentences.text: Yo voy a la farmacia y luego al banco.
sentences.translation: I'm going to the pharmacy and then to the bank.

sentences.text: Nosotros vamos al parque a pasear.
sentences.translation: We're going to the park for a walk.

sentences.text: Los niños van a la escuela en autobús.
sentences.translation: The children go to school by bus.

sentences.text: ¿Vamos juntos al supermercado?
sentences.translation: Shall we go to the supermarket together?

grammar.explanation: '¿Vamos?' is a friendly 'shall we go?'; '¡Vamos!' is 'let's go!'.

sentences.text: Primero voy a la estación a comprar un billete.
sentences.translation: First I'm going to the station to buy a ticket.

sentences.text: El museo está cerca de la plaza mayor.
sentences.translation: The museum is near the main square.

sentences.text: Mañana vamos a la playa todo el día.
sentences.translation: Tomorrow we're going to the beach all day.

sentences.text: ¿Vas al aeropuerto en taxi o en metro?
sentences.translation: Are you going to the airport by taxi or metro?

sentences.text: Voy en metro, es más rápido. ¡Date prisa!
sentences.translation: I'm going by metro, it's faster. Hurry up!

chunks.explanation: The command form of 'darse prisa'. You'll hear it constantly.

sentences.text: Vale, ya voy. No quiero llegar tarde.
sentences.translation: Okay, I'm coming. I don't want to be late.

lessons.title: What are you going to do?
lessons.description: Talk about the near future with ir a plus an infinitive.

sentences.text: ¿Qué vas a hacer este fin de semana?
sentences.translation: What are you going to do this weekend?

grammar.explanation: 'ir a' + an infinitive expresses future plans, like English 'going to'.

sentences.text: Voy a quedar con unos amigos.
sentences.translation: I'm going to meet up with some friends.

words.meaning: to meet up, to arrange to meet

sentences.text: Vamos a salir el sábado por la noche.
sentences.translation: We're going to go out on Saturday night.

sentences.text: Seguro que lo vamos a pasar muy bien.
sentences.translation: We're definitely going to have a great time.

chunks.surface: lo vamos a pasar muy bien
chunks.meaning: we're going to have a great time
chunks.explanation: From 'pasarlo bien' (to have a good time). The 'lo' has no literal meaning here; it's fixed.

sentences.text: El próximo mes voy a visitar a mi familia.
sentences.translation: Next month I'm going to visit my family.

sentences.text: Esta noche vamos a ver una película en casa.
sentences.translation: Tonight we're going to watch a film at home.

sentences.text: Después voy a descansar un rato.
sentences.translation: Afterwards I'm going to rest for a while.

sentences.text: En verano vamos a ir de vacaciones a la playa.
sentences.translation: In summer we're going to go on holiday to the beach.

sentences.text: ¿Vas a invitar a Marta a la fiesta?
sentences.translation: Are you going to invite Marta to the party?

sentences.text: Claro, y luego vamos a bailar toda la noche.
sentences.translation: Of course, and then we're going to dance all night.

sentences.text: Tenemos que planear todo con tiempo.
sentences.translation: We have to plan everything in advance.

sentences.text: Vale, va a ser un finde estupendo.
sentences.translation: Okay, it's going to be a great weekend.

words.explanation: Very casual short form of 'fin de semana'.

lessons.title: I want to but I can't
lessons.description: Stem-changing e to ie verbs like querer, pensar and empezar.

sentences.text: Pienso ir al gimnasio esta tarde.
sentences.translation: I'm thinking of going to the gym this afternoon.

grammar.explanation: In many verbs the 'e' becomes 'ie' when stressed: pensar → pienso, piensas, piensa, but pensamos.

sentences.text: Tengo muchas ganas de empezar el nuevo curso.
sentences.translation: I'm really looking forward to starting the new course.

chunks.explanation: 'tener ganas de' + infinitive = to feel like / look forward to doing something.

sentences.text: La reunión empieza a las nueve en punto.
sentences.translation: The meeting starts at nine sharp.

sentences.text: ¿Entiendes la lección de hoy?
sentences.translation: Do you understand today's lesson?

sentences.text: No entiendo bien. Prefiero preguntar al profesor.
sentences.translation: I don't understand well. I'd rather ask the teacher.

sentences.text: Tienes razón, es una buena idea.
sentences.translation: You're right, it's a good idea.

chunks.explanation: Literally 'you have reason'. Standard way to agree.

sentences.text: Cierro la ventana porque hace frío.
sentences.translation: I'm closing the window because it's cold.

sentences.text: Siempre pierdo las llaves del coche.
sentences.translation: I always lose my car keys.

sentences.text: ¿Qué piensas de la clase de español?
sentences.translation: What do you think of the Spanish class?

sentences.text: Pienso que es muy útil y divertida.
sentences.translation: I think it's very useful and fun.

sentences.text: Te recomiendo el libro. Empieza genial.
sentences.translation: I recommend the book. It starts brilliantly.

sentences.text: Empezamos ahora, si quieres.
sentences.translation: We'll start now, if you like.

grammar.explanation: The 'nosotros' form keeps the original 'e': empezamos, not 'empiezamos'.

lessons.title: I can't sleep
lessons.description: Stem-changing o to ue verbs like poder, dormir and volver.

sentences.text: No puedo dormir esta noche.
sentences.translation: I can't sleep tonight.

grammar.explanation: The 'o' becomes 'ue' when stressed: poder → puedo, puedes, puede, but podemos.

sentences.text: ¿Puedes cerrar la puerta, por favor?
sentences.translation: Can you close the door, please?

grammar.explanation: 'poder' + an infinitive expresses ability or a polite request.

sentences.text: Vuelvo a casa muy tarde los viernes.
sentences.translation: I come home very late on Fridays.

sentences.text: No encuentro mi teléfono por ninguna parte.
sentences.translation: I can't find my phone anywhere.

sentences.text: ¿Cuánto cuesta la entrada del cine?
sentences.translation: How much does the cinema ticket cost?

sentences.text: Los niños juegan y sueñan todo el día.
sentences.translation: The children play and dream all day.

words.explanation: 'jugar' is the only verb where 'u' becomes 'ue': juego, juegas, juega.

sentences.text: Normalmente almuerzo a las dos.
sentences.translation: I usually have lunch at two.

sentences.text: No recuerdo su nombre. ¿Y tú?
sentences.translation: I don't remember his name. Do you?

sentences.text: Estoy agotado. No puedo más.
sentences.translation: I'm exhausted. I can't take any more.

chunks.meaning: I can't take any more, I've had enough
chunks.explanation: From 'no poder más'. Used when you're worn out or fed up.

sentences.text: ¿Puedo probar un poco de tu postre?
sentences.translation: Can I try a bit of your dessert?

sentences.text: Creo que va a llover. Mira el cielo.
sentences.translation: I think it's going to rain. Look at the sky.

sentences.text: Volvemos pronto, ¿vale?
sentences.translation: We'll come back soon, okay?

lessons.title: Ordering at the restaurant
lessons.description: A full restaurant scene with stem-changing e to i verbs.

sentences.text: Buenas noches. ¿Tienen una mesa para dos?
sentences.translation: Good evening. Do you have a table for two?

sentences.text: ¿Tienen reserva? Sí, a nombre de García.
sentences.translation: Do you have a reservation? Yes, under the name García.

sentences.text: ¿Qué me recomienda? Yo pido siempre lo mismo.
sentences.translation: What do you recommend? I always order the same thing.

grammar.explanation: Some -ir verbs change 'e' to 'i': pedir → pido, pides, pide, but pedimos.

sentences.text: De primero, una sopa. De segundo, pollo.
sentences.translation: For starters, a soup. For the main, chicken.

chunks.meaning: for the first course, to start

sentences.text: Yo pido una ensalada y agua con gas.
sentences.translation: I'll have a salad and sparkling water.

sentences.text: El camarero sirve el vino enseguida.
sentences.translation: The waiter serves the wine right away.

sentences.text: ¿Puede repetir, por favor? No he oído bien.
sentences.translation: Could you repeat that, please? I didn't hear you.

sentences.text: De postre, un helado de chocolate.
sentences.translation: For dessert, a chocolate ice cream.

sentences.text: ¡Está para chuparse los dedos!
sentences.translation: It's absolutely delicious!

chunks.explanation: Literally 'to lick one's fingers'. Said about really tasty food.

sentences.text: Todo delicioso. ¿Nos trae la cuenta?
sentences.translation: Everything delicious. Could you bring us the bill?

sentences.text: ¿Se puede pagar con tarjeta?
sentences.translation: Can I pay by card?

sentences.text: Por supuesto. ¿Quieren algo para llevar?
sentences.translation: Of course. Would you like anything to take away?

lessons.title: My daily routine
lessons.description: Describe your daily routine with reflexive verbs.

sentences.text: Me levanto a las siete todos los días.
sentences.translation: I get up at seven every day.

grammar.explanation: Reflexive verbs need a pronoun: me levanto, te levantas, se levanta. It marks the action on yourself.

sentences.text: Primero me ducho y luego me visto.
sentences.translation: First I shower and then I get dressed.

sentences.text: Me lavo la cara y me cepillo los dientes.
sentences.translation: I wash my face and brush my teeth.

sentences.text: Mi hermana se peina el pelo con calma.
sentences.translation: My sister combs her hair calmly.

sentences.text: ¡Venga, ponte las pilas! Vamos a llegar tarde.
sentences.translation: Come on, get a move on! We're going to be late.

chunks.meaning: get a move on, buckle down
chunks.explanation: From 'ponerse las pilas', literally 'put in your batteries' = get energised / get moving.

sentences.text: ¿A qué hora te acuestas normalmente?
sentences.translation: What time do you usually go to bed?

sentences.text: Me acuesto sobre las once y media.
sentences.translation: I go to bed around half past eleven.

sentences.text: Los niños se lavan las manos antes de comer.
sentences.translation: The children wash their hands before eating.

sentences.text: Nos sentamos a la mesa a las dos.
sentences.translation: We sit down at the table at two.

sentences.text: ¿Cómo te llamas y a qué te dedicas?
sentences.translation: What's your name and what do you do?

chunks.meaning: what do you do for a living

sentences.text: Los fines de semana me quedo en casa.
sentences.translation: At weekends I stay at home.

sentences.text: Me relajo, veo una serie y no hago nada.
sentences.translation: I relax, watch a series and do nothing.

lessons.title: How do I get there?
lessons.description: Ask for and give directions around town.

sentences.text: Perdone, ¿cómo llego a la plaza mayor?
sentences.translation: Excuse me, how do I get to the main square?

chunks.explanation: Formal form used with strangers; informal is 'perdona'.

sentences.text: Siga todo recto hasta el semáforo.
sentences.translation: Go straight ahead until the traffic light.

words.explanation: Formal command of 'seguir'.

sentences.text: Luego gire a la izquierda en la esquina.
sentences.translation: Then turn left at the corner.

sentences.text: Cruce la calle y siga hasta el puente.
sentences.translation: Cross the street and go on to the bridge.

sentences.text: Está a la derecha, enfrente del banco.
sentences.translation: It's on the right, opposite the bank.

sentences.text: ¿Está lejos? No, está a dos pasos.
sentences.translation: Is it far? No, it's just around the corner.

chunks.meaning: very close, just around the corner
chunks.explanation: Literally 'two steps away'. Means something is very near.

sentences.text: La primera calle a la derecha es la mía.
sentences.translation: The first street on the right is mine.

sentences.text: El museo está en la segunda manzana.
sentences.translation: The museum is on the second block.

words.explanation: 'manzana' means both 'apple' and a city 'block'.

sentences.text: ¿Voy en metro o puedo ir andando?
sentences.translation: Should I take the metro or can I walk?

chunks.meaning: to go on foot, to walk

sentences.text: Puede ir andando, está cerca de aquí.
sentences.translation: You can walk, it's near here.

sentences.text: Muchas gracias, muy amable.
sentences.translation: Thank you very much, that's very kind.

sentences.text: De nada. ¡Que le vaya bien!
sentences.translation: You're welcome. Take care!

lessons.title: Shopping for clothes
lessons.description: Buy clothes, ask about sizes and prices.

sentences.text: Busco una camisa blanca para el trabajo.
sentences.translation: I'm looking for a white shirt for work.

sentences.text: ¿Qué talla usa? La cuarenta, creo.
sentences.translation: What size do you take? Forty, I think.

sentences.text: ¿Puedo probarme estos pantalones?
sentences.translation: Can I try on these trousers?

sentences.text: El probador está al fondo, a la derecha.
sentences.translation: The fitting room is at the back, on the right.

sentences.text: Esta chaqueta me queda un poco grande.
sentences.translation: This jacket is a bit big on me.

grammar.explanation: 'quedar' like 'gustar' describes how clothes fit: me queda bien/grande/pequeño.

sentences.text: Este vestido te queda muy bien.
sentences.translation: This dress suits you very well.

sentences.text: ¿Cuánto cuesta? ¡Cuesta un ojo de la cara!
sentences.translation: How much is it? It costs a fortune!

chunks.meaning: a fortune, an arm and a leg
chunks.explanation: Literally 'an eye from your face'. Used when something is very expensive.

sentences.text: Pero está de rebajas, cuarenta por ciento menos.
sentences.translation: But it's on sale, forty percent off.

sentences.text: Me llevo estos zapatos del número treinta y ocho.
sentences.translation: I'll take these shoes in size thirty-eight.

words.explanation: For shoes Spanish uses 'número'; for clothes, 'talla'.

sentences.text: ¿Y esta camiseta? Es muy bonita.
sentences.translation: And this T-shirt? It's very nice.

sentences.text: Prefiero la otra. Esta es demasiado cara.
sentences.translation: I prefer the other one. This one is too expensive.

sentences.text: Vale, me lo llevo. ¿Puedo pagar con tarjeta?
sentences.translation: Okay, I'll take it. Can I pay by card?

lessons.title: What's the weather like?
lessons.description: Talk about the weather and the seasons.

sentences.text: ¿Qué tiempo hace hoy?
sentences.translation: What's the weather like today?

grammar.explanation: Spanish uses 'hacer' for weather: hace calor, hace frío, hace sol, hace viento.

sentences.text: Hoy hace mucho calor y hace sol.
sentences.translation: Today it's very hot and sunny.

sentences.text: En invierno hace frío y hace mucho viento.
sentences.translation: In winter it's cold and very windy.

sentences.text: Está nublado y creo que va a llover.
sentences.translation: It's cloudy and I think it's going to rain.

grammar.explanation: Some weather uses 'estar': está nublado, está despejado.

sentences.text: ¡Está lloviendo a cántaros!
sentences.translation: It's pouring down!

chunks.explanation: 'llover a cántaros', literally 'to rain by the jugful' = to rain very heavily.

sentences.text: En primavera llueve mucho pero hace buen tiempo.
sentences.translation: In spring it rains a lot but the weather is nice.

sentences.text: En otoño hay muchas hojas en la calle.
sentences.translation: In autumn there are lots of leaves on the street.

sentences.text: En las montañas nieva en invierno.
sentences.translation: In the mountains it snows in winter.

sentences.text: Hoy estamos a treinta grados. ¡Qué calor!
sentences.translation: Today it's thirty degrees. So hot!

chunks.explanation: For temperature Spanish says 'estamos a X grados'.

sentences.text: Me encanta el verano y el buen tiempo.
sentences.translation: I love summer and the good weather.

sentences.text: A mí no, prefiero el frío del invierno.
sentences.translation: Not me, I prefer the cold of winter.

sentences.text: Mañana va a hacer sol otra vez, por suerte.
sentences.translation: Tomorrow it's going to be sunny again, luckily.

lessons.title: What are you doing right now?
lessons.description: Say what is happening right now with the present progressive.

sentences.text: ¿Qué estás haciendo ahora mismo?
sentences.translation: What are you doing right now?

grammar.explanation: 'estar' + the gerund (-ando/-iendo) describes an action happening right now.

sentences.text: Estoy trabajando, tengo mucho que hacer.
sentences.translation: I'm working, I have a lot to do.

sentences.text: Los niños están comiendo en la cocina.
sentences.translation: The children are eating in the kitchen.

grammar.explanation: -ar verbs make -ando (hablando); -er/-ir verbs make -iendo (comiendo, viviendo).

sentences.text: Estamos esperando el autobús bajo la lluvia.
sentences.translation: We're waiting for the bus in the rain.

sentences.text: Ana está leyendo una novela muy buena.
sentences.translation: Ana is reading a really good novel.

words.explanation: Irregular gerund: leer → leyendo (the 'i' becomes 'y' between vowels).

sentences.text: El bebé está durmiendo, no hagas ruido.
sentences.translation: The baby is sleeping, don't make noise.

words.explanation: Irregular gerund: dormir → durmiendo.

sentences.text: ¿Qué está preparando tu madre para la cena?
sentences.translation: What is your mother making for dinner?

sentences.text: Estoy buscando las llaves, no las encuentro.
sentences.translation: I'm looking for the keys, I can't find them.

sentences.text: Estoy limpiando la casa, está todo sucio.
sentences.translation: I'm cleaning the house, everything is dirty.

sentences.text: Ese ruido me está poniendo nervioso.
sentences.translation: That noise is getting on my nerves.

sentences.text: ¡Estoy hasta las narices de esperar!
sentences.translation: I'm sick and tired of waiting!

chunks.explanation: 'estar hasta las narices (de)', literally 'up to the nose'. Means completely fed up.

sentences.text: Tranquilo, espera un rato más.
sentences.translation: Relax, wait a little longer.

lessons.title: I know how, I know her
lessons.description: The difference between saber (facts) and conocer (people and places).

sentences.text: ¿Sabes dónde está la estación?
sentences.translation: Do you know where the station is?

grammar.explanation: Use 'saber' for facts, information and how to do things.

sentences.text: No lo sé, pero conozco un atajo.
sentences.translation: I don't know, but I know a shortcut.

words.explanation: Irregular yo form: conozco.

grammar.explanation: Use 'conocer' for being familiar with people, places and things.

sentences.text: ¿Sabes cocinar paella?
sentences.translation: Do you know how to cook paella?

grammar.explanation: 'saber' + an infinitive means 'to know how to do something'.

sentences.text: Sí, sé cocinar, pero no muy bien.
sentences.translation: Yes, I know how to cook, but not very well.

sentences.text: ¿Conoces a mi amiga Lucía?
sentences.translation: Do you know my friend Lucía?

grammar.explanation: Put 'a' before a person who is the object: 'conozco a Ana'.

sentences.text: No, no la conozco. ¿Cómo es?
sentences.translation: No, I don't know her. What's she like?

sentences.text: Es muy simpática. Sabe mucho de arte.
sentences.translation: She's really nice. She knows a lot about art.

sentences.text: ¿Conocéis Madrid? Es una ciudad increíble.
sentences.translation: Do you know Madrid? It's an incredible city.

sentences.text: No sabemos su dirección. ¿La sabes tú?
sentences.translation: We don't know his address. Do you know it?

sentences.text: Ni idea. No tengo ni idea, la verdad.
sentences.translation: No clue. I have no idea, honestly.

chunks.meaning: I have no idea, no clue
chunks.explanation: 'no tener ni idea', literally 'not to have even an idea'. Very common for 'I've no clue'.

sentences.text: A lo mejor Ana lo sabe. Vamos a preguntar.
sentences.translation: Maybe Ana knows. Let's ask.

chunks.explanation: Colloquial 'maybe'. Followed by the indicative, not the subjunctive.

sentences.text: Seguro que ella lo sabe. Siempre sabe todo.
sentences.translation: She definitely knows. She always knows everything.

lessons.title: I'll take it
lessons.description: Use direct object pronouns to avoid repeating the noun.

sentences.text: Me gusta esta camisa. Me la llevo.
sentences.translation: I like this shirt. I'll take it.

grammar.explanation: 'lo/la/los/las' replace the thing already mentioned: la camisa → la llevo.

sentences.text: ¿Compras el pan? Sí, lo compro ahora.
sentences.translation: Are you buying the bread? Yes, I'm buying it now.

sentences.text: ¿Tienes las entradas? Sí, las tengo aquí.
sentences.translation: Do you have the tickets? Yes, I have them here.

sentences.text: ¿Ves a los niños? No, no los veo.
sentences.translation: Can you see the children? No, I can't see them.

grammar.explanation: The object pronoun goes just before the conjugated verb, after 'no'.

sentences.text: Voy a comprarlo mañana en el centro.
sentences.translation: I'm going to buy it tomorrow downtown.

grammar.explanation: With an infinitive you can attach the pronoun to the end: comprarlo, verlo, hacerlo.

sentences.text: ¿Necesitas la bolsa? Sí, la necesito.
sentences.translation: Do you need the bag? Yes, I need it.

sentences.text: ¿Me das el recibo? Sí, aquí lo tienes.
sentences.translation: Will you give me the receipt? Yes, here it is.

sentences.text: El cambio, ¿lo quiere en billetes?
sentences.translation: The change, do you want it in notes?

sentences.text: ¿Puede envolverlo? Es un regalo.
sentences.translation: Can you wrap it? It's a gift.

sentences.text: ¿Pago con esta tarjeta? Sí, la acepto.
sentences.translation: Shall I pay with this card? Yes, I accept it.

sentences.text: ¿Prefieres pagar en efectivo? Como quieras.
sentences.translation: Do you prefer to pay in cash? Whatever you like.

chunks.meaning: whatever you like, up to you

sentences.text: Perfecto. ¡Trato hecho!
sentences.translation: Perfect. It's a deal!

chunks.explanation: Literally 'deal done'. Said to close an agreement, like 'deal!' in English.

lessons.title: I'll tell you
lessons.description: Indirect object pronouns for saying to whom you do something.

sentences.text: Te digo la verdad: no me gusta nada.
sentences.translation: I'm telling you the truth: I don't like it at all.

grammar.explanation: These say to/for whom: me (to me), te (to you), le (to him/her), les (to them).

sentences.text: Le doy un regalo a mi madre.
sentences.translation: I'm giving a present to my mother.

grammar.surface: Le doy un regalo a mi madre
grammar.meaning: I give a gift to my mother
grammar.explanation: 'le' is often clarified with 'a + person', even though it seems repeated.

sentences.text: ¿Me explicas la lección otra vez?
sentences.translation: Will you explain the lesson to me again?

sentences.text: Claro, te la explico ahora mismo.
sentences.translation: Sure, I'll explain it to you right now.

grammar.explanation: The indirect pronoun comes first: te (to you) + la (it) → te la explico.

sentences.text: Mis padres me mandan dinero cada mes.
sentences.translation: My parents send me money every month.

sentences.text: ¿Me prestas tu bolígrafo un momento?
sentences.translation: Will you lend me your pen for a moment?

sentences.text: Le pregunto al profesor si tengo una duda.
sentences.translation: I ask the teacher if I have a question.

sentences.text: ¿Puedes echarme una mano con esto?
sentences.translation: Can you give me a hand with this?

chunks.meaning: give me a hand, help me
chunks.explanation: 'echar una mano' = to help. Here 'me' shows who is helped.

sentences.text: Por supuesto, te ayudo con mucho gusto.
sentences.translation: Of course, I'll help you gladly.

sentences.text: Nos dan buenas noticias sobre el trabajo.
sentences.translation: They give us good news about the job.

sentences.text: Te doy un buen consejo: no digas nada.
sentences.translation: I'll give you good advice: don't say anything.

sentences.text: Vale, te debo un favor.
sentences.translation: Okay, I owe you a favour.

lessons.title: Bigger, smaller, better
lessons.description: Compare things with comparatives.

sentences.text: Mi coche es más rápido que el tuyo.
sentences.translation: My car is faster than yours.

grammar.explanation: 'más ... que' makes comparisons of superiority: más alto que, más caro que.

sentences.text: Este ejercicio es menos difícil que el otro.
sentences.translation: This exercise is less difficult than the other one.

grammar.explanation: 'menos ... que' compares inferiority: menos caro que, menos rápido que.

sentences.text: El tren es tan cómodo como el autobús.
sentences.translation: The train is as comfortable as the bus.

grammar.explanation: 'tan ... como' expresses equality: tan alto como, tan fácil como.

sentences.text: Esta película es mejor que la anterior.
sentences.translation: This film is better than the previous one.

grammar.explanation: Some are irregular: bueno → mejor, malo → peor, grande → mayor, pequeño → menor.

sentences.text: El tiempo hoy es peor que ayer.
sentences.translation: The weather today is worse than yesterday.

sentences.text: Mi hermano es mayor que yo, tiene treinta.
sentences.translation: My brother is older than me, he's thirty.

sentences.text: El metro es más barato y más práctico.
sentences.translation: The metro is cheaper and more practical.

sentences.text: Este libro es más interesante que aquel.
sentences.translation: This book is more interesting than that one.

sentences.text: Ana trabaja tanto como su jefe.
sentences.translation: Ana works as much as her boss.

grammar.explanation: With verbs use 'tanto como': trabaja tanto como yo.

sentences.text: Nuestra casa es igual de grande que la vuestra.
sentences.translation: Our house is just as big as yours.

sentences.text: Bueno, llegas tarde, pero más vale tarde que nunca.
sentences.translation: Well, you're late, but better late than never.

chunks.explanation: A common saying, used exactly like its English twin.

sentences.text: Tienes razón, lo importante es llegar.
sentences.translation: You're right, the important thing is to arrive.

lessons.title: The best in the city
lessons.description: Talk about the best and worst with superlatives, and give opinions.

sentences.text: Este es el mejor restaurante de la ciudad.
sentences.translation: This is the best restaurant in the city.

grammar.surface: el mejor restaurante de la ciudad
grammar.meaning: the best restaurant in the city
grammar.explanation: Use 'el/la más + adjective + de' for the superlative. 'de' means 'in/of'.

sentences.text: Es la película más famosa del año.
sentences.translation: It's the most famous film of the year.

sentences.text: En mi opinión, es el peor día de la semana.
sentences.translation: In my opinion, it's the worst day of the week.

sentences.text: Este café está buenísimo. ¡Qué rico!
sentences.translation: This coffee is really delicious. So good!

grammar.explanation: Add '-ísimo' for 'very/extremely': bueno → buenísimo, caro → carísimo.

sentences.text: Para mí, el verano es la mejor estación.
sentences.translation: For me, summer is the best season.

words.explanation: 'estación' means both 'season' and 'station'.

sentences.text: Es el lugar más popular del barrio.
sentences.translation: It's the most popular place in the neighbourhood.

sentences.text: Este sitio es especial, es mi favorito.
sentences.translation: This place is special, it's my favourite.

sentences.text: La comida aquí es la más rica del mundo.
sentences.translation: The food here is the tastiest in the world.

sentences.text: Ese hotel es carísimo, pero es genial.
sentences.translation: That hotel is really expensive, but it's great.

sentences.text: El examen es facilísimo, es pan comido.
sentences.translation: The exam is super easy, it's a piece of cake.

chunks.meaning: a piece of cake, very easy
chunks.explanation: Literally 'eaten bread'. Means something is very easy.

sentences.text: ¡Qué bien! Entonces vamos a aprobar.
sentences.translation: Great! Then we're going to pass.

sentences.text: Seguro que sí. Somos los mejores.
sentences.translation: Definitely. We're the best.

lessons.title: Whose is this?
lessons.description: Talk about who owns things with full possessives.

sentences.text: ¿De quién es este paraguas?
sentences.translation: Whose umbrella is this?

grammar.explanation: To ask who owns something, use '¿de quién es...?'

sentences.text: Es mío. Gracias por encontrarlo.
sentences.translation: It's mine. Thanks for finding it.

grammar.explanation: After 'ser', use the stressed forms: mío, tuyo, suyo, nuestro, vuestro.

sentences.text: Estas gafas son tuyas, ¿verdad?
sentences.translation: These glasses are yours, right?

sentences.text: No, no son mías. Creo que son suyas.
sentences.translation: No, they're not mine. I think they're hers.

sentences.text: Nuestra casa es pequeña, pero la vuestra es enorme.
sentences.translation: Our house is small, but yours is huge.

grammar.explanation: 'nuestro/a' = our; 'vuestro/a' = your (plural in Spain). They agree with the noun.

sentences.text: ¿Es tuya esta mochila azul?
sentences.translation: Is this blue backpack yours?

sentences.text: Sí, es mi mochila. Dentro está mi cartera.
sentences.translation: Yes, it's my backpack. My wallet is inside.

sentences.text: En la oficina de objetos perdidos hay un abrigo.
sentences.translation: In the lost property office there's a coat.

sentences.text: Ese abrigo no es suyo, es del señor de al lado.
sentences.translation: That coat isn't his, it's the man's next door.

sentences.text: ¿Y esta maleta? Es nuestra, es del viaje.
sentences.translation: And this suitcase? It's ours, it's from the trip.

sentences.text: Yo prefiero hacer las cosas a mi manera.
sentences.translation: I prefer to do things my own way.

chunks.explanation: Literally 'in my manner'. Means doing something the way you want.

sentences.text: Cada uno tiene su propio estilo.
sentences.translation: Everyone has their own style.

lessons.title: This one or that one?
lessons.description: Point things out with demonstratives.

sentences.text: Este libro es mío y ese es tuyo.
sentences.translation: This book is mine and that one is yours.

grammar.explanation: 'este' = near me; 'ese' = near you; 'aquel' = far from both of us.

sentences.text: Aquella casa al final de la calle es muy antigua.
sentences.translation: That house at the end of the street is very old.

sentences.text: ¿Qué es esto? No lo entiendo.
sentences.translation: What's this? I don't understand it.

grammar.explanation: Use 'esto/eso/aquello' for something with no clear gender or that you can't name yet.

sentences.text: Eso que dices no tiene sentido.
sentences.translation: What you're saying doesn't make sense.

sentences.text: Prefiero estos zapatos, no esos.
sentences.translation: I prefer these shoes, not those.

sentences.text: ¿Ves aquellas montañas allí a lo lejos?
sentences.translation: Do you see those mountains over there in the distance?

sentences.text: Dame ese de ahí, el que está en la mesa.
sentences.translation: Give me that one over there, the one on the table.

sentences.text: ¿Quieres este trozo o prefieres otro?
sentences.translation: Do you want this piece or would you rather another?

sentences.text: Quiero un poco de aquello, se ve delicioso.
sentences.translation: I want a bit of that, it looks delicious.

sentences.text: ¿Prestas atención a lo que digo?
sentences.translation: Are you paying attention to what I'm saying?

sentences.text: ¿Volver allí otra vez? ¡Ni hablar!
sentences.translation: Go back there again? No way!

chunks.meaning: no way!, out of the question!
chunks.explanation: Literally 'not even to talk'. A firm refusal.

sentences.text: Vale, vale, no pasa nada. Elige tú.
sentences.translation: Okay, okay, no problem. You choose.

lessons.title: A lot or very much?
lessons.description: The difference between muy and mucho, plus other quantifiers.

sentences.text: Estoy muy cansada y tengo mucho sueño.
sentences.translation: I'm very tired and very sleepy.

grammar.explanation: 'muy' goes before adjectives/adverbs; 'mucho' goes before nouns or after verbs.

sentences.text: Trabajo mucho, pero gano muy poco.
sentences.translation: I work a lot, but I earn very little.

grammar.explanation: After a verb, 'mucho' never changes: trabajo mucho, como mucho.

sentences.text: Hay demasiada gente en el metro hoy.
sentences.translation: There are too many people on the metro today.

words.explanation: 'gente' is singular in Spanish: la gente es...

sentences.text: Tengo bastante trabajo esta semana.
sentences.translation: I have quite a lot of work this week.

sentences.text: Este piso es muy pequeño, hay poco espacio.
sentences.translation: This flat is very small, there's little space.

sentences.text: Casi no tengo tiempo para nada.
sentences.translation: I hardly have time for anything.

sentences.text: Hay mucho ruido, no tengo suficiente calma.
sentences.translation: There's a lot of noise, I don't have enough peace.

sentences.text: Comes demasiado rápido, come más despacio.
sentences.translation: You eat too fast, eat more slowly.

grammar.explanation: Before an adverb, 'demasiado' stays unchanged: demasiado rápido, demasiado tarde.

sentences.text: Gasto mucho dinero, pero apenas ahorro.
sentences.translation: I spend a lot of money, but I barely save.

sentences.text: Tienes tanta energía. ¿Cómo lo haces?
sentences.translation: You have so much energy. How do you do it?

sentences.text: Estás muy estresado. No es para tanto.
sentences.translation: You're very stressed. It's not that big a deal.

chunks.meaning: it's not that big a deal
chunks.explanation: Used to calm someone down, meaning 'it's not such a big deal'.

sentences.text: Tienes razón, necesito descansar un poco más.
sentences.translation: You're right, I need to rest a little more.

lessons.title: Do you want to come?
lessons.description: Make plans, invite people and accept or turn down invitations.

sentences.text: ¿Te apetece ir al cine esta noche?
sentences.translation: Do you fancy going to the cinema tonight?

grammar.explanation: 'apetecer' works like 'gustar': te apetece un café, te apetece salir.

sentences.text: ¡Vale! ¿Quedamos a las ocho?
sentences.translation: Sure! Shall we meet at eight?

grammar.explanation: 'quedar' means to arrange to meet: quedamos a las ocho, quedo con Ana.

sentences.text: ¿Por qué no vamos a tomar algo antes?
sentences.translation: Why don't we go for a drink first?

grammar.explanation: '¿Por qué no...?' is a friendly way to suggest something.

chunks.meaning: to have a drink, grab something

sentences.text: Buena idea. Podemos ir a esa cafetería nueva.
sentences.translation: Good idea. We can go to that new café.

sentences.text: ¿Quieres venir a mi fiesta el sábado?
sentences.translation: Do you want to come to my party on Saturday?

sentences.text: Me encantaría, pero el sábado estoy ocupado.
sentences.translation: I'd love to, but I'm busy on Saturday.

chunks.explanation: A polite way to accept or to soften a refusal.

sentences.text: ¡Qué pena! ¿Y el domingo estás libre?
sentences.translation: What a shame! And are you free on Sunday?

sentences.text: El domingo sí. ¿Vamos a un concierto?
sentences.translation: Sunday, yes. Shall we go to a concert?

sentences.text: Perfecto, acepto encantada. Compro las entradas.
sentences.translation: Perfect, I gladly accept. I'll buy the tickets.

sentences.text: ¿Te apetece cenar después del concierto?
sentences.translation: Do you fancy dinner after the concert?

sentences.text: La verdad es que no me apetece nada salir hoy.
sentences.translation: Honestly, I really don't feel like going out today.

chunks.meaning: I really don't feel like it
chunks.explanation: Adding 'nada' makes it emphatic: 'I don't feel like it at all'.

sentences.text: No te preocupes, quedamos otro día.
sentences.translation: Don't worry, we'll meet another day.

lessons.title: A table for two
lessons.description: Make polite requests and handle problems at a restaurant.

sentences.text: Buenas noches, ¿tienen mesa para dos?
sentences.translation: Good evening, do you have a table for two?

sentences.text: Quiero reservar para las nueve, por favor.
sentences.translation: I'd like to book for nine, please.

sentences.text: ¿Me pone una botella de agua, por favor?
sentences.translation: Could you bring me a bottle of water, please?

grammar.meaning: could you bring me a bottle
grammar.explanation: '¿me pone...?' is a very common polite way to order in Spain.

sentences.text: ¿Podría traernos la carta, por favor?
sentences.translation: Could you bring us the menu, please?

words.explanation: 'la carta' is the à la carte menu; 'el menú' is often the set meal.

grammar.explanation: '¿Podría...?' is the most polite request form, using the conditional.

sentences.text: Perdone, falta un tenedor en la mesa.
sentences.translation: Excuse me, there's a fork missing on the table.

grammar.explanation: 'faltar' works like 'gustar': falta un tenedor, faltan dos platos.

sentences.text: También necesitamos un cuchillo y una cuchara.
sentences.translation: We also need a knife and a spoon.

sentences.text: Este plato está frío. ¿Me lo puede cambiar?
sentences.translation: This dish is cold. Can you change it for me?

sentences.text: Claro, ahora mismo se lo traigo caliente.
sentences.translation: Of course, I'll bring it to you hot right away.

grammar.explanation: Before lo/la/los/las, 'le' becomes 'se': se lo traigo, se la doy.

sentences.text: ¿Nos falta algo más? Sí, otra servilleta.
sentences.translation: Are we missing anything else? Yes, another napkin.

sentences.text: ¿Me pasas la sal, por favor?
sentences.translation: Will you pass me the salt, please?

sentences.text: Todo estupendo. La cuenta, por favor.
sentences.translation: Everything great. The bill, please.

sentences.text: ¿Pagamos a medias? Vale, mitad y mitad.
sentences.translation: Shall we split it? Okay, half and half.

chunks.explanation: 'ir/pagar a medias' = to split something 50/50, especially a bill.

lessons.title: It hurts here
lessons.description: Talk about aches and symptoms at the doctor's.

sentences.text: Doctor, me duele mucho la cabeza.
sentences.translation: Doctor, my head really hurts.

grammar.explanation: 'doler' works like 'gustar': me duele (singular), me duelen (plural). Use 'la', not 'mi'.

sentences.text: ¿Desde cuándo? Desde hace dos días.
sentences.translation: Since when? For two days now.

grammar.explanation: 'desde hace' + a period says how long something has been going on.

sentences.text: También me duelen la espalda y las piernas.
sentences.translation: My back and legs hurt too.

sentences.text: ¿Tiene fiebre? Sí, y también tengo tos.
sentences.translation: Do you have a fever? Yes, and I also have a cough.

sentences.text: Creo que tengo un resfriado o algo así.
sentences.translation: I think I have a cold or something like that.

sentences.text: Me duele la garganta al hablar.
sentences.translation: My throat hurts when I speak.

sentences.text: Le voy a dar una receta para una medicina.
sentences.translation: I'm going to give you a prescription for some medicine.

sentences.text: Tiene que descansar y beber mucha agua.
sentences.translation: You have to rest and drink lots of water.

sentences.text: También me duele un poco el estómago.
sentences.translation: My stomach also hurts a little.

sentences.text: La verdad es que estoy hecho polvo.
sentences.translation: Honestly, I'm completely worn out.

chunks.explanation: 'estar hecho polvo', literally 'made into dust'. Means exhausted or in bad shape. A woman says 'hecha polvo'.

sentences.text: Con esta medicina va a mejorar pronto.
sentences.translation: With this medicine you'll get better soon.

sentences.text: Gracias, doctor. Cuídese usted también.
sentences.translation: Thank you, doctor. You take care too.

lessons.title: Doing the chores
lessons.description: Talk about housework and whose turn it is.

sentences.text: Hoy me toca limpiar la cocina.
sentences.translation: Today it's my turn to clean the kitchen.

grammar.explanation: 'tocar' like 'gustar': me toca a mí = it's my turn.

sentences.text: Acabo de fregar todos los platos.
sentences.translation: I've just washed all the dishes.

grammar.explanation: 'acabar de' + infinitive means to have just done something.

sentences.text: ¿Puedes sacar la basura, por favor?
sentences.translation: Can you take out the rubbish, please?

sentences.text: Tengo que lavar y planchar la ropa.
sentences.translation: I have to wash and iron the clothes.

sentences.text: Mi hermano barre el suelo del salón.
sentences.translation: My brother sweeps the living room floor.

sentences.text: Después ordeno mi dormitorio y hago la cama.
sentences.translation: Then I tidy my bedroom and make the bed.

sentences.text: ¿Quién cocina hoy? Cocino yo, si quieres.
sentences.translation: Who's cooking today? I'll cook, if you like.

sentences.text: Nos toca hacer la compra esta tarde.
sentences.translation: It's our turn to do the shopping this afternoon.

sentences.text: Hay demasiada tarea en casa esta semana.
sentences.translation: There are too many chores at home this week.

sentences.text: Pero, mira, la casa está como los chorros del oro.
sentences.translation: But look, the house is spotless.

chunks.explanation: Literally 'like streams of gold'. Means perfectly clean and shining.

sentences.text: ¡Qué bien! Ahora podemos descansar.
sentences.translation: Great! Now we can rest.

sentences.text: Sí, acabamos de terminar todo. Menos mal.
sentences.translation: Yes, we've just finished everything. Thank goodness.

lessons.title: Getting around town
lessons.description: Take public transport and manage tickets and timetables.

sentences.text: Voy al trabajo en metro todos los días.
sentences.translation: I go to work by metro every day.

grammar.explanation: Use 'en' with transport: en metro, en tren, en coche. But 'a pie' = on foot.

sentences.text: ¿Cuánto tarda el autobús en llegar?
sentences.translation: How long does the bus take to arrive?

grammar.explanation: 'tardar en' + infinitive says how long something takes: tarda diez minutos en llegar.

sentences.text: Necesito un billete de ida y vuelta.
sentences.translation: I need a return ticket.

sentences.text: El tren sale del andén número tres.
sentences.translation: The train leaves from platform three.

sentences.text: Hay un retraso de veinte minutos, qué rollo.
sentences.translation: There's a twenty-minute delay, what a pain.

chunks.meaning: what a drag, what a pain
chunks.explanation: Colloquial complaint about something boring or annoying.

sentences.text: ¿Cojo el taxi o espero el próximo tren?
sentences.translation: Shall I take the taxi or wait for the next train?
